one_liner: A 71-year-old asplenic male from Nantucket presents in summer with high fevers, rigors, drenching sweats, jaundice, and dark urine, with blood smear showing intraerythrocytic ring forms and a 'Maltese cross' tetrad.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient vacations annually on Nantucket Island, Massachusetts and has been there for the past 3 weeks. Five days ago, he developed gradual onset of malaise, anorexia, and myalgias, followed by high fevers (up to 40°C) with drenching sweats and shaking chills. Over the past 2 days, he noticed darkening of his urine (tea-colored) and yellowing of his skin. He reports a mild non-productive cough and headache. He does not recall any tick bites but spent significant time gardening and walking through tall grass. He had a splenectomy 15 years ago after a motor vehicle accident. He has not traveled internationally and has not received a blood transfusion. No history of malaria. It is currently July.
stage2.pmh: Asplenia (post-traumatic splenectomy 15 years ago), atrial fibrillation, hypertension, coronary artery disease (status post PCI 4 years ago).
stage2.surgical_history: Splenectomy (15 years ago, traumatic splenic rupture), PCI with stent placement (4 years ago).
stage2.medications: Apixaban 5 mg BID, metoprolol succinate 50 mg daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, aspirin 81 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily.
stage2.social_history: Retired attorney. Summers on Nantucket Island, MA. Non-smoker. Moderate wine consumption (1-2 glasses daily). No illicit drugs. Lives with wife.
stage2.physical_exam: Acutely ill-appearing elderly male with visible jaundice and rigors. Scleral icterus. No conjunctival pallor initially apparent. Lungs: bibasilar crackles. Heart: irregularly irregular rhythm, no murmurs. Abdomen: well-healed left upper quadrant surgical scar consistent with prior splenectomy. No hepatomegaly. No lymphadenopathy. Skin: jaundiced, no rashes, no erythema migrans, no petechiae. Neurologic: oriented x3, mildly lethargic but follows commands. No focal deficits. Dark urine in Foley bag.

lab_results.result: Intraerythrocytic ring forms with multiple rings per RBC. Occasional 'Maltese cross' (tetrad) forms identified. Parasitemia calculated at 12%.
lab_results.clinical_significance: The Maltese cross (tetrad) is pathognomonic for Babesia and distinguishes it from Plasmodium; high parasitemia (>10%) in asplenic patients indicates severe disease
Confirmatory molecular diagnosis with species identification; more sensitive than smear at low parasitemia
Severe hemolytic anemia from intraerythrocytic parasitization and immune-mediated destruction
Thrombocytopenia from splenic sequestration (normally, but asplenic here) and consumptive process; common in this condition
WBC usually normal or slightly low in this condition; elevated Howell-Jolly bodies noted on smear confirming asplenia
Predominantly indirect hyperbilirubinemia from intravascular and extravascular hemolysis
Elevated reticulocyte count reflecting bone marrow response to hemolytic anemia
Acute kidney injury from hemoglobinuria, hemodynamic compromise, and direct parasitic endothelial effects
Co-infection with Borrelia burgdorferi occurs in 10-20% of Babesia cases (same Ixodes tick vector); requires Western blot confirmation
Excludes anaplasmosis co-infection (also transmitted by Ixodes scapularis)

imaging_results.result: Mild bilateral interstitial edema. No focal consolidation. Cardiomegaly.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Mild pulmonary edema likely from volume overload and hemodynamic stress; ARDS can complicate severe this condition

acceptable_tests: Babesia serology (IgM/IgG) — may be negative early in acute infection
Lyme serology and Anaplasma PCR (co-infection screening from same tick vector)
low_yield_tests: Malaria RDT (will not detect Babesia; species-specific antigen)

stage5.final_clue: An asplenic elderly male from a Babesia-endemic area (Nantucket, summer) with high fevers, rigors, severe hemolytic anemia (Hgb 8.2, undetectable haptoglobin, LDH 1240, indirect bilirubin 6.8), thrombocytopenia, AKI, and jaundice. Blood smear shows intraerythrocytic ring forms with the pathognomonic Maltese cross (tetrad) at 12% parasitemia. PCR confirms Babesia microti. The presence of asplenia, parasitemia >10%, hemoglobin <10, and renal insufficiency defines severe this condition requiring exchange transfusion.

debrief.classic_presentation: A febrile illness in a patient from a Babesia-endemic area (coastal New England — Nantucket, Martha's Vineyard, Long Island, Connecticut, coastal New Jersey) during summer months (May-September). Symptoms include high fever, rigors, drenching sweats, myalgias, headache, and anorexia. Severe disease with hemolytic anemia, jaundice, renal failure, and ARDS occurs in high-risk populations: asplenic patients, the elderly (>50 years), and immunocompromised individuals (HIV, malignancy, anti-TNF therapy). The combination of asplenia + babesiosis is particularly dangerous with mortality rates of 20-40%.
debrief.attending_mode_notes: The Maltese cross (tetrad) is pathognomonic for Babesia and is the single finding that definitively distinguishes it from Plasmodium falciparum on blood smear. However, tetrads are seen in only ~20% of cases, and the ring forms of Babesia and P. falciparum can be nearly identical. Key smear differences from malaria: Babesia has no gametocytes, no schizonts, no hemozoin (malaria pigment), and extracellular merozoites are more common. Travel history is the most reliable initial discriminator (Nantucket vs. Sub-Saharan Africa). Co-infection with Lyme and/or Anaplasma occurs in 10-20% of Babesia cases since Ixodes scapularis transmits all three pathogens. Co-infected patients have more severe and prolonged illness. Babesia can also be transmitted via blood transfusion (now screened for in the US) and is the most common transfusion-transmitted parasite in the US. Exchange transfusion criteria: parasitemia ≥10%, severe hemolysis (Hgb <10 g/dL), renal/hepatic/pulmonary compromise, or altered mental status. This patient meets multiple criteria. For relapsing babesiosis in immunocompromised patients, prolonged therapy (≥6 weeks, with last 2 weeks after parasitemia clears) is required.
debrief.pathophysiology: Babesia microti (Northeast US) and B. duncani (Pacific Coast) are intraerythrocytic apicomplexan parasites transmitted by the Ixodes scapularis tick. The natural reservoir is the white-footed mouse (Peromyscus leucopus). After tick inoculation of sporozoites, parasites invade erythrocytes and undergo asexual replication (budding, not schizogony as in Plasmodium), forming merozoites that lyse the host RBC and invade new erythrocytes. The spleen is the primary defense against babesiosis: splenic macrophages pit (remove) intraerythrocytic parasites without destroying the RBC and filter heavily parasitized cells. Asplenic patients cannot clear parasitized RBCs, leading to exponential parasitemia increase and massive intravascular hemolysis. Hemolysis causes hemoglobinemia, hemoglobinuria, jaundice, and acute kidney injury from hemoglobin cast nephropathy. Unlike P. falciparum, Babesia does not sequester in capillaries (no cerebral malaria equivalent), but severe cases can develop ARDS, DIC, and multiorgan failure.
debrief.treatment_overview: Mild-moderate disease (parasitemia <4%, no organ dysfunction): atovaquone 750 mg PO BID + azithromycin 500 mg PO day 1, then 250 mg daily for 7-10 days. Severe disease (this patient): IV azithromycin 500 mg daily + atovaquone 750 mg PO BID + exchange transfusion. Alternative for severe disease: IV clindamycin 600 mg Q8h + quinine 650 mg PO TID for 7-10 days (more side effects: cinchonism, hypoglycemia, QTc prolongation). Exchange transfusion (red cell exchange, targeting parasitemia <5%): indicated for parasitemia ≥10%, severe hemolysis, renal/hepatic/pulmonary failure, or DIC. Monitor smears daily to document parasitemia clearance. For Lyme co-infection: add doxycycline 100 mg BID for 14-21 days. Immunocompromised patients may require prolonged therapy (≥6 weeks, including 2 weeks after smear clearance) due to high relapse rates.
high_yield_pearls: The Maltese cross (tetrad) on blood smear is pathognomonic for Babesia — this finding definitively distinguishes it from malaria.
Asplenic patients are at extreme risk for severe and fatal babesiosis — asplenia + babesiosis has a mortality rate of 20-40%.
Babesia, Borrelia burgdorferi (Lyme), and Anaplasma phagocytophilum are all transmitted by Ixodes scapularis — always screen for co-infections when one is diagnosed.
Exchange transfusion is indicated for parasitemia ≥10%, Hgb <10 g/dL, or organ dysfunction (renal, hepatic, pulmonary) — it rapidly reduces parasite burden and removes hemolysis byproducts.
Babesiosis is the most common transfusion-transmitted parasitic infection in the US — donor screening was implemented nationally in 2024.
sources: Krause PJ et al. Clinical Practice Guidelines by the Infectious Diseases Society of America (IDSA): 2020 Guideline on Diagnosis and Management of Babesiosis. Clin Infect Dis. 2021;72(2):e53-e120.
Vannier E, Krause PJ. Human babesiosis. N Engl J Med. 2012;366(25):2397-2407.
UpToDate: Babesiosis — Clinical manifestations, diagnosis, treatment, and prevention (2024)

after_stage2_more_likely: Babesiosis — febrile hemolytic illness in an asplenic patient from Nantucket in summer with tick exposure; geography and host factors are classic
Anaplasmosis — also transmitted by Ixodes in this geography, causes fever and cytopenias; can be co-infection
after_stage2_less_likely: Malaria — no international travel to endemic areas; cannot be excluded on clinical grounds alone but epidemiology makes it very unlikely
Autoimmune hemolytic anemia — would not explain fever with rigors or the acute geographic/seasonal pattern
after_labs_top3: Babesiosis — pathognomonic Maltese cross (tetrad) on smear, 12% parasitemia, PCR confirms B. microti, massive hemolysis, asplenia, classic endemic area and season
Malaria — ring forms can mimic Plasmodium, but Maltese cross is specific for Babesia, no travel history, and PCR confirms Babesia
Anaplasmosis co-infection — excluded by negative Anaplasma PCR; Lyme co-infection possible (borderline Lyme ELISA)
